en.extract:
The Taveuni silktail (Lamprolia victoriae) is a species of bird endemic to Fiji. This beautiful bird looks superficially like a diminutive bird-of-paradise but it is actually closely related to the fantails.
Finsch named the species after Victoria, eldest daughter of Queen Victoria, who was then Crown Princess of Germany.


Description
The Taveuni silktail is a small black bird, measuring around 12 centimetres (4.7 in) and weighing 16 to 21 g (0.56–0.74 oz). It is a small, dumpy bird with long rounded wings, and a short rounded tail. The plumage of the male is velvet black with metallic blue iridescent spangling on the crown and breast, and silky white lower-back patch that travels most of the length down the tail. The margin of the tail is black, sometimes this tip has the same iridescence as other parts of the body. The female is similar to the male, except less glossy, and immature birds are duller than the adults and may have buffy rumps and backs. The irises of this species are dark, and the legs and bill are blackish. The bill is heavy and slightly hooked at the end. The legs are long and the feet strong.


Distribution and habitat
The Taveuni silktail is endemic to forests of Taveuni in Fiji. It occurs widely. It occurs in mature wet rainforests, as well as forest patches, and is also found in human-modified habitats such as logged forests and in plantations near patches of natural forest.


Behaviour
It can be quite elusive and difficult to see, but may also be confiding and approachable. It is usually very active at dawn, and is encountered either singly or in small flocks. 
The diet consists mainly of insects, worms and arthropods.


Status and conservation

The Taveuni silktail is a restricted range species. Forest clearance for mahogany plantations and general forest clearance within its range have led to the species being listed as vulnerable in 1994; however, this was downgraded to near threatened in 2006. While its habitat is being lost, it is not yet severely fragmented. The species occurs within the Ravilevu Nature Reserve and the Bouma National Heritage Park.

The '''Taveuni silktail''' ('''''Lamprolia victoriae''''') is a species of bird [[endemism|endemic]] to [[Fiji]]. This beautiful bird looks superficially like a diminutive [[bird-of-paradise]] but it is actually closely related to the [[fantail]]s.

Finsch named the species after [[Victoria, Princess Royal|Victoria]], eldest daughter of [[Victoria of the United Kingdom|Queen Victoria]], who was then Crown Princess of Germany.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Finsch|first=Otto|year=1873|title=On Lamprolia victoriae, a most remarkable Passerine Bird from the Feejee Islands|journal=Proceed. of the Zool. Society of London Pt. 3|pages=733–735|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/90422#page/885/}}</ref>

==Description==
The Taveuni silktail is a small black bird, measuring around {{convert|12|cm|in}} and weighing {{convert|16|to(-)|21|g|oz|abbr=on}}. It is a small, dumpy bird with long rounded wings, and a short rounded tail. The [[plumage]] of the male is velvet black with metallic blue [[iridescence|iridescent]] spangling on the crown and breast, and silky white lower-back patch that travels most of the length down the tail. The margin of the tail is black, sometimes this tip has the same iridescence as other parts of the body. The female is similar to the male, except less glossy, and immature birds are duller than the adults and may have buffy rumps and backs. The irises of this species are dark, and the legs and bill are blackish.<ref name = "HBW">{{Cite book   | first1 = Brian   | last1 = Coates   | first2 = Guy   | last2 = Dutson   | first3 = Chris   | last3 = Filardi   | first4 = Peter   | last4 = Clement   | first5 = Phil   | last5 = Gregory   | first6 = Kees   | last6 = Moeliker  |author-link6=Kees Moeliker | editor-last = Josep   | editor-first =  del Hoyo   | editor2-last = Andrew   | editor2-first =  Elliott   | editor3-last = David   | editor3-first = Christie   | contribution = Family Monarchidae (Monarch-flycatchers)  | title = Handbook of the Birds of the World. Volume 11, Old World Flycatchers to Old World Warblers  | year = 2007  | page = 322  | place = Barcelona  | publisher = Lynx Edicions  | isbn = 978-84-96553-06-4   }}</ref> The bill is heavy and slightly hooked at the end. The legs are long and the feet strong.<ref name="Emu">{{cite journal|last=Cottrell |first=G.W.|year=1966|title=A problem species: Lamprolia victoriae.|journal=Emu|volume=66|issue=3|pages=253–266 |doi = 10.1071/MU966253 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The Taveuni silktail is [[Endemism in birds|endemic]] to forests of [[Taveuni]] in [[Fiji]]. It occurs widely. It occurs in mature wet rainforests, as well as forest patches, and is also found in human-modified habitats such as logged forests and in plantations near patches of natural forest.<ref name = "HBW"/>

==Behaviour==
It can be quite elusive and difficult to see, but may also be confiding and approachable. It is usually very active at dawn, and is encountered either singly or in small flocks. 
 
The diet consists mainly of insects, worms and [[arthropods]].

==Status and conservation==
[[File:Silktail vidawa jun08a.ogv|thumbtime=20|thumb|300px|Vidawa, Taveuni, Fiji Isles ([[golden whistler]] calling in background)]]
The Taveuni silktail is a restricted range species. Forest clearance for [[mahogany]] plantations and general forest clearance within its range have led to the species being listed as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] in 1994; however, this was downgraded to [[near threatened]] in 2006. While its habitat is being lost, it is not yet severely fragmented. The species occurs within the [[Ravilevu Nature Reserve]] and the Bouma National Heritage Park.<ref name = "HBW"/>
